repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

comment_data.body: This PR makes condition a little bit messy. I was thinking that if we want use attributes/methods for testing, we should not allow to have type as `None` because in that case we don't know where we forget to test `None` before accessing attribute. We should create `UnknownType` class with all attributes/methods. This should allow us to test safely the types in conditions...
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -225,16 +225,18 @@ def _optimise_for_loop(self, node, iterable, reversed=False):
             if annotation.is_subscript:
                 annotation = annotation.base  # container base type
 
-        if Builtin.dict_type in (iterable.type, annotation_type):
+        if (iterable.type and iterable.type.is_dict_type) or (annotation_type and annotation_type.is_dict_type):

comment_data.body: I think we tend to call these a lot more than we create the types, so bare attributes would be more efficient than properties.

comment_data.body:
Looking through the usages, I wonder if additional flags like `is_anyset_type` and `is_tuple_or_list_type` would be helpful. Maybe a `@property` could work for these cases, although it shouldn't be much effort to set them at type creation time.

And while we're at it, why not an `is_builtin_sequence_type` as well …

comment_data.body: I am 99% sure but I would like to ask to be sure. Can I also replace occurrences of `is py_object_type` with attribute test `.is_pyobject` ? The `is` test check whether the type *is* `PyObjectType` instance, the latter tests whether it is `PyObjectType` instance or instance of it subclass. Can I replace it automatically or I need to check case-by-case? Not 100% sure.

These are really different things (unfortunately). Any Python object type has `.is_pyobject == True`, so this is used to distinguish Python types from C types, whereas `type is py_object_type` is commonly used to detect if we know a specific type or have plain `object` as (unknown Python) type here.

I was afraid that it is not so simple. Based on discussion elsewhere should not we have more explicit tests like `.is_python_type` and `.is_plain_pyobject` - or something like that... I would not put it in this PR because I would like to move on with subscription PR which depends on this...

Or if you could lead me - does it make sense to replace all `is py_object_type` check with some attribute access I can do it in this PR if it is just simple (dump) replacement. I would like to ask also what attribute name to avoid back-and-forth discussion about name...

Since the "is plain/unknown object" test is checking for an exact type, I think `is py_object_type` is as explicit as it gets, and also unlikely to pose a problem in the future.

It's unfortunate that `is_pyobject` and `is_pyint` / `is_pylist` do different things now. I suggest to change it one more time (sorry) to `is_pyint_type` etc. to make it clearer that we're interested in a specific type, whereas `is_pyobject` is _not_ testing for a specific type but a generic type feature (like `is_int` etc.).

(I can see that that was my original proposal in https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7543#issuecomment-3970739394)

> I suggest to change it one more time (sorry) to is_pyint_type etc. to make it clearer that we're interested in a specific type,

Done. Marking as resolved.
This could be a candidate for an `is_builtin_sequence` (which can be added later, if we feel like it).

comment_data.body:
I see this pattern a lot:  `is_pyxxx_type or is py_object_type`. What is usually meant here is "may be pyxxx type", in the sense that if it's really known to be `xxx`, or if we don't know anything about the (Python) type (i.e. it's `object`), then it might be of type `xxx`. If it's another known type, then definitely not.

Could this be worth secondary properties, `may_be_pyxxx_type` ?

Let's give the assertion a helpful error message if it ever fails.
```suggestion
            assert self.type.is_pylist_type, self.type
```

-            assert self.type is list_type
+            assert self.type.is_pylist_type

comment_data.body:
EDIT: Turns out, half of this condition is actually dead code since we never set `annotation_type` to anything but `None`. And since we now support parsing annotations properly into types, it's completely useless code and we can remove the whole annotation related code section above. So this is really just
```python
if iterable.type.is_pyset_type or iterable.type.is_pyfrozenset_type:
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -225,16 +225,18 @@ def _optimise_for_loop(self, node, iterable, reversed=False):
             if annotation.is_subscript:
                 annotation = annotation.base  # container base type
 
-        if Builtin.dict_type in (iterable.type, annotation_type):
+        if (iterable.type and iterable.type.is_pydict_type) or (annotation_type and annotation_type.is_pydict_type):
             # like iterating over dict.keys()
             if reversed:
                 # CPython raises an error here: not a sequence
                 return node
             return self._transform_dict_iteration(
                 node, dict_obj=iterable, method=None, keys=True, values=False)
 
-        if (Builtin.set_type in (iterable.type, annotation_type) or
-                Builtin.frozenset_type in (iterable.type, annotation_type)):
+        if (
+            (iterable.type and (iterable.type.is_pyset_type or iterable.type.is_pyfrozenset_type)) or
+            (annotation_type and (annotation_type.is_pyset_type or annotation_type.is_pyfrozenset_type))
+        ):

This should have a prefix, too. For consistency, but also to prevent confusion with Cython's memoryviews.


comment_data.body: Hmm there is up to ~10 occurrences I was able to grep from the whole Cython codebase. So not sure if it is worth it. If you still want to have it I can implement it easily.

Both of the tests above would remain correct with `is_pymemoryview_type` added - they're saying "slicing returns the same type" which is also true for `memoryview`.

Edit: "both" is wrong - there's a third test above that I missed. But it looks like it'd also be correct if it included memoryview
I think `may_be_pyxxx_type` expresses the intent more clearly than `is_pyxxx_type or is py_object_type`. For me, that alone makes it worse adding the properties.
OK thank you @da-woods for your help. I implemented the changes then. I also renamed `is_sequence` to `is_builtin_sequence`.
Fixed.

commit.message: Introduce attributes to test builtin types list, tuple, set, frozenset, dict
Use attributes instead of operator is for builtin types
prefix builtin type attributes with py

title: Refactor of tests of the builtin types
